caveats: a: No public occupation-by-industry table was found for the newly defined 2022 six-digit code.
a: The estimate excludes work already automated in current POS, e-commerce, and inventory systems.
a: The code mixes traditional tobacco, cigar, vape, and online sellers with different advice and handling intensity.
rho: This is an implementation judgment, not a measured adoption rate.
rho: State and local licensing, flavor restrictions, and delivery rules vary materially.
rho: Tools that improve advice or marketing may raise service quality without reducing paid hours.
e: The supplied firm-count primitive is a declared dataset gap, so this share cannot produce a defensible eligible-firm count.
e: Public data do not identify the share of LMM firms dependent on unauthorized vape products.
e: The 2022 code absorbed some electronic-shopping and other direct-selling activity, increasing channel heterogeneity.
s5: The owner-age source is economy-wide, dated 2018, and counts responding owners rather than firms.
s5: No denominator-consistent 459991 control-transfer series was found.
s5: Regulatory and product-legality risk can turn apparent succession supply into closures rather than transfers.
q: No causal evidence links AI labor savings to five-year tobacco-specialty retail margins.
q: Excise taxes and manufacturer pricing can swamp an operator's controllable cost advantage.
q: The estimate concerns retention of implemented gross benefit, not demand growth or implementation success.
d5: Consumer prevalence is not purchase quantity, and dollar sales are not constant-price demand.
d5: National product trends include channels outside 459991, especially convenience stores.
d5: Rapid regulation and substitution across nicotine formats make extrapolation unusually uncertain.
o: Accountable operator need does not imply that today's independent owner or location retains the sale.
o: Online age-verification law and enforcement can change during the horizon.
o: The lower bound incorporates channel displacement, not demand decline, which is reserved for d5.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Predominantly consumer-paid merchandise revenue at posted retail prices, with repeat purchases of consumables and devices; ancillary delivery, loyalty, and product-advice functions support transactions rather than separate fees.
lens.excluded_activities: Shells, captive outlets, non-control financing vehicles, owner-only microshops below the EBITDA band, convenience stores classified elsewhere, pure manufacturers or wholesalers, stores dependent on unlawful products, and operations without transferable systems, leases, licenses, inventory controls, and customer relationships.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market operators of tobacco, cigar, vape, and smoking-supplies specialty stores or compliant online storefronts whose recurring customer proposition combines assortment, product guidance, age-controlled fulfillment, and repeat replenishment.

a.bridge: BLS and O*NET describe retail-sales and cashier work across retail industries, not the 459991 occupation mix. I map language-heavy product questions, promotion communication, payment exception handling, reconciliation, and inventory administration to AI-assisted or partly substitutable work, then give greater weight to the physical and regulated duties of specialty tobacco retail. The proxy likely overstates exposure because 459991 requires more face-to-face interaction, age control, physical stocking, and product handling than generic task text reveals.
caveats: No public occupation-by-industry table was found for the newly defined 2022 six-digit code.
The estimate excludes work already automated in current POS, e-commerce, and inventory systems.
The code mixes traditional tobacco, cigar, vape, and online sellers with different advice and handling intensity.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted employee roster for multi-store tobacco and vape retailers, paired with time-and-motion logs by task and current POS, inventory, CRM, and loss-prevention automation, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: Retail work combines automatable information and transaction tasks with irreducibly physical and accountable tasks. BLS lists recommending products, explaining use, answering questions, processing payment, tracking inventory, and stocking; O*NET reports daily face-to-face discussion for 88% of retail salespersons and gives cashier duties that include reconciliation, stocking, and shipment preparation. AI can draft answers and promotions, assist demand planning and reconciliation, and reduce routine support effort, but cannot independently perform much of store-floor handling or lawful sale authorization.

d5.bridge: CDC measures adult product-use prevalence and national manufacturer or broad-channel product sales, not constant-price, constant-quality demand at 459991 specialty retailers. I treat falling cigarette use and pack sales as a negative component and rising e-cigarette and oral-nicotine demand as offsets, then bound a five-year basket ratio around modest contraction. The proxy likely understates specialty-store demand where vape and novel nicotine products over-index, but it may overstate lawful demand if enforcement or flavor restrictions disproportionately remove specialty-shop assortment.
caveats: Consumer prevalence is not purchase quantity, and dollar sales are not constant-price demand.
National product trends include channels outside 459991, especially convenience stores.
Rapid regulation and substitution across nicotine formats make extrapolation unusually uncertain.
d5.change_evidence: Circana or Nielsen specialty-channel unit and inflation-adjusted sales by cigarettes, cigars, vape, oral nicotine, accessories, and legal authorization status through 2026, plus state flavor-ban cohorts, would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: The basket is shifting rather than disappearing. CDC found exclusive cigarette prevalence declined from 10.8% in 2017 to 7.9% in 2023 while exclusive e-cigarette prevalence rose from 1.2% to 4.1%; it also reports cigarette-pack sales down 27% from 2015 to 2021, nicotine lozenge, puck, and pouch sales more than doubling from 2020 to 2022, and monthly e-cigarette sales rising from $75 million in January 2017 to $469 million in March 2022. The base therefore assumes legacy declines are largely, but not fully, offset by newer formats over the next five years.

caveats: The supplied firm-count primitive is a declared dataset gap, so this share cannot produce a defensible eligible-firm count.
Public data do not identify the share of LMM firms dependent on unauthorized vape products.
The 2022 code absorbed some electronic-shopping and other direct-selling activity, increasing channel heterogeneity.
e.change_evidence: A census of brokers' marketed and closed 459991 businesses with normalized EBITDA, product-authority status, lease assignability, license transferability, owner dependence, and multi-store systems would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: Most firms large enough to fall in the specified EBITDA band should be operating retail businesses with repeat purchases and transferable locations, systems, inventory, and customer traffic. The haircut removes mixed retailer-manufacturers, businesses reliant on unauthorized products, highly owner-dependent boutiques, weak lease or license positions, and firms whose economics cannot be normalized. The official NAICS definition confirms that the industry is composed of specialty retailers, including tobacco, cigar, e-cigarette, and vape shops.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 44-45 ($651,293,900,629) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 44-45 ($6,850,896,411,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: ancestor 44-45. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1011 (margins.json[45]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Retail (General) EBITDA/Sales 10.11%); revenue/employee $430,267 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 44-45); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: Accountable operator need does not imply that today's independent owner or location retains the sale.
Online age-verification law and enforcement can change during the horizon.
The lower bound incorporates channel displacement, not demand decline, which is reserved for d5.
o.change_evidence: Five-year cohort data on specialty-store share of legal tobacco and nicotine sales, online penetration, autonomous-checkout adoption, age-verification exception rates, store closures, and consumer switching to convenience or mass retail would materially change this estimate.
o.rationale: Software can enable online ordering, recommendations, marketing, inventory decisions, and some checkout, but lawful fulfillment still requires a regulated retailer and accountable controls. FDA inspects physical retailers, investigates online retailers, and enforces under-21 sales restrictions, so online migration changes the channel more readily than it eliminates the operator. The principal displacement risk is share moving to larger online, convenience, or mass-market operators rather than pure software supplying the basket.

caveats: No causal evidence links AI labor savings to five-year tobacco-specialty retail margins.
Excise taxes and manufacturer pricing can swamp an operator's controllable cost advantage.
The estimate concerns retention of implemented gross benefit, not demand growth or implementation success.
q.change_evidence: Store-level gross-margin and price-panel data for matched SKUs before and after labor-saving deployments, including competitor prices, supplier rebates, promotion depth, traffic, and renewal or loyalty behavior over several years, would materially change the range.
q.rationale: Merchandise is sold at posted prices rather than under cost-plus or mandatory pass-through contracts, so an operator can initially retain labor savings. Over five years, however, transparent product prices, nearby convenience and specialty stores, online sellers, loyalty discounts, and supplier bargaining should pass part of the benefit to customers or vendors. Retention should remain meaningful where advice, compliant assortment, convenience, and local availability differentiate the store.

caveats: This is an implementation judgment, not a measured adoption rate.
State and local licensing, flavor restrictions, and delivery rules vary materially.
Tools that improve advice or marketing may raise service quality without reducing paid hours.
rho.change_evidence: Pilot results from at least ten multi-location specialty retailers, reporting baseline and post-deployment paid hours by workflow, exception rates, age-verification failures, shrink, compliance findings, and 12-month adoption persistence, would materially move this range.
rho.rationale: Over five years, common cloud POS, inventory, CRM, scheduling, marketing, and support tools should make a meaningful share of the exposed work implementable. Realization is capped by fragmented small-firm data, employee turnover, integration cost, and the need for a person to remain accountable for age checks, authorized-product assortment, cash, theft, and compliance. FDA inspections of physical retailers and surveillance of online retailers make unsupervised automation especially risky.

s5.bridge: The Census Annual Business Survey figure covers responding owners of 4.1 million U.S. employer businesses in 2018, not 459991 owners, and measures age rather than completed control transfers. I use the reported 51% age-55-plus share only as a succession-pressure anchor, then apply a large reduction for owners who continue working, close, transfer internally without a qualifying control event, or cannot sell. The age proxy likely overstates qualifying transfers because retirement timing is delayed and many retail operations are not saleable as going concerns.
caveats: The owner-age source is economy-wide, dated 2018, and counts responding owners rather than firms.
No denominator-consistent 459991 control-transfer series was found.
Regulatory and product-legality risk can turn apparent succession supply into closures rather than transfers.
s5.change_evidence: Industry-coded five-year closed-deal counts from BizBuySell, Axial, DealStats, or broker CRM data, matched to the stock of 459991 LMM firms and classified by external control sale versus closure, family transfer, or recapitalization, would materially change this estimate.
s5.rationale: A five-year qualifying transfer probability in the high teens is plausible for an owner-operated specialty-retail population, but direct industry deal incidence was not found. Census reported that 51% of responding employer-business owners were 55 or older, supporting succession pressure while not establishing sale timing or transferability.

principal_driver: Repeat consumable purchases plus standardizable sales, inventory, support, and administrative workflows provide the main operating lever.
principal_weakness: Low labor intensity and volatile regulation constrain the economic impact, while the eligible-firm denominator is missing.

run_meta.title: Tobacco, Electronic Cigarette, and Other Smoking Supplies Retailers

sources.supports: Defines 459991 as establishments primarily retailing cigarettes, electronic cigarettes, cigars, tobacco, pipes, and other smokers' supplies; examples include cigar and cigarette retailers, tobacco retailers, e-cigarette retailers, smokers' supply retailers, and vape shops.
sources.title: 2022 North American Industry Classification System Manual — Industry 459991

sources.supports: Lists duties including customer assistance, product recommendation and explanation, answering questions, payment processing, promotions, stocking, delivery arrangements, and inventory tracking; projects little or no employment change from 2024 to 2034.
sources.title: Bureau of Labor Statistics Occupational Outlook Handbook: Retail Sales Workers

sources.supports: Reports that 88% of respondents have face-to-face discussions every day and 95% rate dealing with external customers as very or extremely important, supporting the human-interaction constraint.
sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Retail Salespersons (41-2031.00)

sources.supports: Describes payment processing and duties including reconciling total payments, helping customers, returns, stocking, cleaning, packaging, and carry-out service.
sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Cashiers (41-2011.00)

sources.supports: Documents FDA compliance inspections of physical tobacco retailers, surveillance and investigations of online retailers, under-21 sales enforcement, and retailer responsibility for applicable federal tobacco rules.
sources.title: FDA: Retail Sales of Tobacco Products

sources.supports: Finds exclusive cigarette prevalence fell from 10.8% to 7.9% while exclusive e-cigarette prevalence rose from 1.2% to 4.1% between 2017 and 2023.
sources.title: CDC MMWR: Trends in Adult Commercial Tobacco Product Use, United States, 2017–2023

sources.supports: Reports cigarette-pack sales down about 27% from 2015 to 2021, nicotine lozenge, puck, and pouch sales more than doubling from 2020 to 2022, and monthly e-cigarette sales increasing from $75 million in January 2017 to $469 million in March 2022.
sources.title: CDC: Economic Trends in Tobacco

sources.supports: Reports that 51% of responding owners of employer businesses were age 55 or older in the 2019 Annual Business Survey (data year 2018), with the stated responding-owner and employer-business limitations.
sources.title: U.S. Census Bureau: Business Owners' Ages

title: Tobacco, Electronic Cigarette, and Other Smoking Supplies Retailers
